nativeRange.habitat: Clear, shallow, fast-flowing hill streams and their margins; substrates of sand, gravel, and rounded rocks with leaf litter; riparian vegetation provides shade and tannin-stained water in some localities

decorPreferences: smooth rounded pebbles and fine gravel substrate
driftwood and leaf litter for tannins and cover
moderate to strong current from powerhead or filter outlet

diet.notes: Accepts high-quality micro-pellets, finely crushed flake, and frozen or live micro-foods: daphnia, baby brine shrimp, cyclops, and micro-worms. Small mouth size means food particle size matters; standard flake should be crushed before feeding. A varied diet with regular live or frozen micro-foods brings out best coloration.

goodWith: other small peaceful danios (D. erythromicron, D. margaritatus)
dwarf shrimp (Neocaridina, Caridina) in planted tanks
avoidWith: large or boisterous fish that outcompete at feeding

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase reference image for Danio choprae; shows typical copper-body with blue-green iridescent lateral stripe. Verify availability before use.
Wikimedia Commons image; check current license and photographer attribution on the file page before use.

summary: A jewel-sized Myanmar endemic danio bearing a copper-gold body overlaid with a striking blue-green iridescent lateral stripe. Originally described in genus Celestichthys, it was synonymised into Danio in 2008. Cool-water requirements (20–25°C) and schooling nature make it a standout for unheated nano and species tanks.
careNotes:
The Glowlight Danio (Danio choprae) is endemic to the Hkamti region of the upper Chindwin River drainage in northern Myanmar. It inhabits clear, well-oxygenated hill streams, and this origin dictates its most distinctive husbandry demand: cool water. A temperature range of 20–25°C is recommended; temperatures consistently above 26°C shorten lifespan and suppress immune function. In many climates an aquarium chiller or a cool basement setting is needed in summer — supplemental heating is rarely required. A tight-fitting lid is recommended as danios are strong jumpers.

Keep in schools of at least 8 (larger groups of 12+ produce noticeably more active shoaling behaviour and reduce any minor chasing). The species is entirely peaceful toward tankmates of similar size. Its small mouth — typical of nano danios — limits food particle size; crush standard flakes or use purpose-sized micro-pellets.

Genus note: D. choprae was originally described as Celestichthys choprae (Kullander & Fang, 2004) and later placed in its own genus. Tyson Roberts' 2007 revision subsumed Celestichthys into Danio (published 2008), making Danio choprae the current valid name. The common name 'Glowlight Danio' can cause confusion with the unrelated Glowlight Tetra (Hemigrammus erythrozonus, family Characidae) — these are entirely different fish from different continents and families.

Water quality should be pristine: soft to moderately hard (gH 2–12), slightly acidic to neutral (pH 6.5–7.5), with low nitrates. Regular partial water changes of 25–30% weekly are advisable. Gentle or moderate flow replicating the hill-stream origin is appreciated; a powerhead or spray-bar outlet works well.
breedingNotes: Egg scatterer; spawning is not difficult to trigger but fry are small and require careful rearing. Condition a mixed-sex group with live or frozen micro-foods for 1–2 weeks. Move adults to a separate spawning tank (25–40 L) with fine-leaf plants (Java moss, hornwort) or a spawning mop over a mesh bottom to protect eggs from being eaten. A temperature of 22–24°C and slightly acidic, very soft water encourages spawning. Adults will eat their own eggs; remove adults after spawning. Eggs hatch in approximately 48–72 hours at 24°C; fry become free-swimming 2–3 days later. First foods: infusoria, commercial fry food (Hikari First Bites), or vinegar eels; transition to baby brine shrimp nauplii as they grow. Breedability is rated moderate due to the small fry size and specific temperature and water-softness requirements.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Danio choprae
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided native range (Hkamti, upper Chindwin drainage, Myanmar), adult size (~3.5 cm), temperature 20–25°C, pH 6.5–7.5, hardness 18–179 ppm (approx. gH 1–10), habitat description (clear, shallow, well-oxygenated hill streams), diet, behaviour, and breeding notes. Taxonomy note: Celestichthys choprae synonymised into Danio following Roberts (2007, publ. 2008) confirmed.

additional.name: FishBase — Danio choprae
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Danionidae, Cypriniformes), adult size (max ~3.5 cm TL), native range (Myanmar: Chindwin River system, Hkamti area), temperature range (20–25°C), and confirmed current valid name as Danio choprae with Celestichthys choprae as synonym.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Glowlight Danio
additional.notes: Trade-focused care guide. Confirmed beginner care level, schooling group size of 6+ (8+ recommended for best behaviour), tank size guidance (~55 L / 15 gal minimum for nano schools), diet acceptance of crushed flake and micro-foods, and cool-water requirement. Noted common name confusion with Glowlight Tetra.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Danio choprae species profile
additional.notes: Confirmed Myanmar endemic status, iridescent copper-gold body with blue-green lateral stripe, cool-water preference, and peaceful community temperament. Also corroborated the genus revision from Celestichthys to Danio.

additional.name: Kullander & Fang (2004) — Five new species of Danio from Myanmar — Copeia
additional.notes: Original species description of Celestichthys choprae by Kullander & Fang (2004), Copeia 2004(4):1116–1128. Documents the Hkamti type locality, morphological diagnosis, and holotype data. Referenced for taxonomic accuracy; original genus Celestichthys subsequently synonymised into Danio by Roberts (2007).

fish.breedingNotes: Egg scatterer; spawning achievable in captivity with soft water, fine-leaf plants or spawning mop, and conditioning on live foods. Fry are very small and require infusoria or commercial fry food initially. Remove adults post-spawning to prevent egg predation.
